content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1–3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/2012.02107
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/2012.02107/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1–3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDF→md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it — pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone — proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1–3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1–2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3–5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/2012.02107

Begin by reading paper.md.

1	## Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion
     2	
     3	Xiaoding Yuan 1
     4	
     5	Adam Kortylewski 2
     6	
     7	Yihong Sun 2
     8	
     9	1 Tongji University, 2 Johns Hopkins University
    10	
    11	## Abstract
    12	
    13	Analyzing complex scenes with Deep Neural Networks is a challenging task, particularly when images contain multiple objects that partially occlude each other. Existing approaches to image analysis mostly process objects independently and do not take into account the relative occlusion of nearby objects. In this paper, we propose a deep network for multi-object instance segmentation that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from bounding box supervision only. Our work builds on Compositional Networks, which learn a generative model of neural feature activations to locate occluders and to classify objects based on their non-occluded parts. We extend their generative model to include multiple objects and introduce a framework for efficient inference in challenging occlusion scenarios. In particular, we obtain feed-forward predictions of the object classes and their instance and occluder segmentations. We introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that locates erroneous segmentations and estimates the occlusion order to correct them. The improved segmentation masks are, in turn, integrated into the network in a top-down manner to improve the image classification. Our experiments on the KITTI INStance dataset (KINS) and a synthetic occlusion dataset demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our model at multi-object instance segmentation under occlusion. Code is publically available at https://github.com/XD7479/Multi-Object-Occlusion.
    14	
    15	## 1. Introduction
    16	
    17	Scenes in images most often depict multiple objects that partially occlude each other. Recent studies [38, 18] showed that deep networks are less robust at recognizing partially occluded objects compared to Humans. The main difficulties are raised by the combinatorial variability of the object ordering and positioning, as well as the fact that scenes can contain known and unknown object classes.
    18	
    19	One approach to address the problem of occlusion in deep networks is data augmentation [36, 5, 34, 1]. While this increases the robustness of deep networks, the classification performance on partially occluded objects still remains substantially worse compared to non-occluded objects. Recent work introduced compositional deep networks (CompositionalNets) and showed that these are more robust to partial occlusion compared to data augmentation approaches [16, 17, 30]. CompositionalNets are deep neural network architectures in which the fully connected classification head is replaced with a differentiable compositional model. The structure of the compositional model enables CompositionalNets to decompose images into objects and context, as well as to further decompose objects into their individual parts. The generative nature of the compositional model enables it to segment objects and occluders Alan Yuille 2
    20	
    21	Figure 1: Our proposed model corrects erroneous instance segmentations through multi-object reasoning. Left: Two input images that are processed independently. The segmentation results identify visible object parts in blue, invisible parts in red, and context in green. Note how in the top image, the model cannot identify the occlusion. Center: By enforcing consistency between segmentations of nearby objects, our model can identify conflicting segmentations (white area). Right: Reasoning about the occlusion order resolves the erroneous predictions.
    22	
    23	<!-- image -->
    24	
    25	[28] and to recognize objects based on their non-occluded parts. However, CompositionalNets, as well as other popular architectures, treat each object in an image independently and do not explicitly exploit the mutual relationship of nearby objects.
    26	
    27	In this paper, we introduce a deep network for multiobject instance segmentation that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from bounding box supervision only. Our work builds on and significantly extends CompositionalNets. Specifically, we extend the generative model in CompositionalNets to allow for instance segmentation of multiple mutually occluding objects in an image. This multiobject generative model is hard to optimize because of the mutual dependencies between objects. To solve this optimization efficiently, we introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that takes as input the independent predictions of each objects label, the instance segmentation and the occluder segmentation (Figure 1). We proceed to estimate possibly erroneous predictions through an occlusion voting mechanism. During occlusion voting, each object in the image votes for every pixel in its bounding box if the pixel is occupied by the object or if is occluded. Pixels which receive ambiguous votes from multiple objects indicate segmentation errors. To correct these we leverage the occlusion order of overlapping bounding boxes based on the classification scores. The corrected instance and occlusion segmentation masks are fed back into the CompositionalNet to mask out those features that induced segmentation errors, and to improve the prediction of the object class.
    28	
    29	We perform extensive experiments on the KITTI INStance dataset (KINS). We further introduce a synthetic dataset that comprises artificially generated images of partially occluded objects, which are generated by superimposing segmented objects from the KITTI. The synthetic generation of partially occluded images enables us to evaluate custom types of occlusion challenges such as: pairwise occlusion, multi-object occlusion and mixed occlusion containing both known and unknown object classes as occluders. Our experimental results highlight that reasoning about multi-object occlusion significantly enhances the robustness of deep networks as it enables them to detect erroneous feed-forward predictions and self-correct through reasoning about multi-object occlusion. In summary, our contributions in this work are:
    30	
    31	1. We introduce a deep network for multi-object instance segmentation that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from bounding box super-vision only. Specifically, our network defines a generative model of multiple objects and achieves enhanced robustness through reasoning about multi-object occlusion.
    32	
    33	2. We introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that enables efficient inference in generative models with multiple objects. In particular, it detects erroneous feed-forward predictions and and corrects them through reasoning about the occlusion order of objects.
    34	
    35	3. We achieve state-of-the-art performance at instance segmentation under occlusion on the KITTI INStance (KINS) dataset.
    36	
    37	4. We introduce an occlusion challenge generated from real-world segmented objects with accurate annotations and propose a taxonomy of occlusion scenarios that pose a particular challenge for computer vision.
    38	
    39	## 2. Related Work
    40	
    41	Occlusion reasoning. A number of approaches have recently been proposed to integrate occlusion reasoning in areas including image classification [17, 32], object detection [30], segmentation [8, 31] and tracking [33]. Gao et al. [8] introduce binary variables to infer the visible cells in a bounding box. Hsiao and Hebert [13] model occlusions by reasoning about 3D relationship of objects approximated by their bounding boxes. Recent works on pixellevel occlusion reasoning include a probabilistic model proposed by George et al. [10] that contains mutual occlusion inference on text-based CAPTCHAs by approximating MAP solution through message passing. Another probabilistic framework by Yang et al. [33] introduce occlusion priori modeled by Markov random field to tackle mutual occlusion in object tracking task. Tighe et al. [29] introduce an inter-class occlusion prior to parse scenes and refine pixellevel labels. OFNet designed by Lu et al. [22] considers the relevance between occlusion contours and pixel orientations, but no semantic information is included. Zhan et al. [35] propose pair-wise order recovery by comparing the amodal mask completion of neighboring objects in a selfsupervised way, while lack the ability of handling unknown occlusion. Our proposed architecture performs pixel-level occlusion reasoning and ensures the consistence of object shape by object-level occlusion order recovery. Note that we can handle both the unknown occlusion and multi-object occlusion at the same time.
    42	
    43	Weakly-supervised instance segmentation. While instance segmentation performance was significantly advanced by CNN based architectures [11, 3, 21, 2], pixellevel semantic annotation is required for training by fully supervised methods. Weakly-supervised segmentation methods require only image-level supervision [27, 23] and bounding-box-level annotations [25, 20] to reduce the cost of dense labeling. DeepCut proposed by Rajchl et al. [25] extends GrabCut [26] by training a CNN as classifier from bounding box annotations and address instance segmentation as energy minimisation problem based on conditional random fields. Zhou et al. [37] present an instance mask extraction by class response maps indicating visual cues with image-level supervision. Hsu et al. [14] address the problem as multiple instance learning task and estimate the foreground/background by generating positive/negative bags based on the sweeping lines of each bounding box. Amodal instance segmentation task were introduced more recently. Li et al. [19] firstly presented a solution for amodal instance segmentation training with artificial occlusion. Other methods [39, 24, 7] implement fully-supervised amodal mask completion. In this work, we build on the weaklysupervised instance segmentation CompositionalNets[28], and generalize them to allow for reasoning about multiobject occlusion.
    44	
    45	Figure 2: Proposed deep network architecture for multi-object instance segmentation under occlusion. Given an input image, we crop objects based on their bounding box. Each crop is processed by a Compositional Network to obtain independent estimates of the object class, instance segmentation and occlusion segmentation. Subsequently, these are processed by the multi-object reasoning module, which detects inconsistent segmentations and corrects them by taking into account the occlusion order of the objects. The corrected instance segmentation mask is used in a top-down manner to mask out occluded features, which, in turn, improves the classification score. Note we draw two Compositional Networks for illustrative purpose, in practice the images are processed sequentially by the same network.
    46	
    47	<!-- image -->
    48	
    49	## 3. Robustness through Occlusion Reasoning
    50	
    51	Notation. The output of the layer l in a DCNN is referred to as feature map F l = ψ ( I, Ω) ∈ R H × W × D , where I and Ω are the input image and the parameters of the feature extractor, respectively. Feature vectors are vectors in the feature map, f l i ∈ R D at position i , where i is defined on the 2D lattice of F l with D being the number of channels in the layer l . We omit subscript l in the following for clarity since the layer l is fixed a priori in the experiments.
    52	
    53	## 3.1. Prior Work: CompNets for Single Objects
    54	
    55	CompositionalNets [16, 17] are deep neural network architectures in which the fully connected classification head is replaced with a differentiable compositional model. In particular, the classification head defines a generative model p ( F | y ) of the features F for an object class y :

Z ( σ k ) is the normalization constant. The priors p ( i | m,y ) and likelihood parameters can be learned by segmenting the training images into foreground and background. We follow the approach introduced in [30] which uses weakly supervised segmentation based on the bounding box annotation to segment the context from the object. All model parameters { Ω , { Θ y }} can be trained end-to-end as discussed in [16, 28].

    85	Partial Occlusion. Compositional networks can be augmented with an outlier model to enhance their robustness to partial occlusion. The intuition is that at each position i in the image either the object model p ( f i |A m i,y , χ m i,y , Λ) or an outlier model p ( f i | β, Λ) is active:

In particular, we can predict the pixel-wise labels to be foreground F , context C or occlusion O by computing the respective likelihoods:

   105	## 3.2. Compositional Networks for Multiple Objects
   106	
   107	The main limitation of Compositional Networks is that they assume only one object is present in an image. They can be trivially generalized to multiple objects by treating each object independently [16, 30]. However, assuming independence between objects neglects the relations between them and leads to inconsistencies in the segmentation results. For example Figure 3 shows how two objects with overlapping bounding boxes both predict that they are visible in the overlapped region. Whereas, it is clear that only one object can be visible per pixel in an image.
   108	
   109	In this work, we aim to resolve such inconsistencies by enabling deep networks to reason about multi-object occlusion. In particular, we generalize the generative model in compositional networks to multiple objects by extending the model likelihood:
   110	
   111	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   112	
   113	with ∑ n z i,n =1 and z i,n ∈{ 0 , 1 } . This generalized likelihood includes n = { 1 , . . . , N } object models p n ( f i )= p ( F | θ m y n , β ) , which correspond to the number of objects in the image, and the outlier model p N +1 ( f i )= p ( f i | β, Λ) . Note that, by the design of the likelihood, only one object model can be active at any location i in the feature map F . Maximizing the model likelihood defined in Equation 12 is difficult because it involves multiple objects and the visibility at each pixel z i,n depends on the visibility of the neighboring pixels. We solve this complex optimization problem by introducing a multi-object reasoning module into the architecture of CompositionalNets.
   114	
   115	## 3.3. Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion
   116	
   117	In this section, we introduce a deep network for multiobject instance segmentation that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from bounding box super-vision only. To make our discussion concise, we constrain ourselves in this section to images that contain two objects, where one partially occludes the other. Note, however, that our model trivially extends to multiple objects.
   118	
   119	Feed-forward extraction of likelihood maps. Our proposed network architecture is illustrated in Figure 2. We draw two CompositionalNet architectures to enhance the clarity of the illustration, in practice they are sequentially processed by the same network. The objects in the input image I are cropped based on their bounding box and first independently processed by a CompositionalNet. For each image crop I 1 , I 2 we obtain a class prediction ˆ y 1 , ˆ y 2 and three likelihood maps that encode the foreground, context and occlusion likelihood in the feature map:

   126	
   127	we compute F 2 , C 2 , O 2 respectively. We illustrate the likelihood maps throughout the paper as a two-dimensional heat map that is color coded. In particular, we visualize at each pixel which likelihood has the highest value by coloring occluder red, foreground blue and context green. The pixel intensity encodes the difference between the three likelihood terms. For dark pixels all likelihoods have similar values, hence indicating that the model is uncertain, whereas at bright pixels one likelihood is clearly higher compared to the other. As shown in Figure 3, instance segmentation based on the likelihood maps can be incorrect in the region Order graph where the two bounding boxes overlap (yellow box), particularly, for the occluded object. In practice, we observe that errors occur most often when an object is occluded by another object of the same category. As discussed earlier, this is caused by the fact that the segmentation is performed independently of other objects in an image, and in addition also per pixel independently. While these independence assumptions enable an efficient feed-forward inference, they neglect important relationships in images. For example in the overlapping region of the bounding boxes in Figure 3, we want all pixels to be assigned to the same object. It is very unnatural to treat every pixel independently. We propose a multi-object reasoning module that resolves such segmentation conflicts by taking into account additional relationships between objects at minimal computational overhead.

We first detect segmentation conflicts as those image pixels are classified as foreground by both object models (Figure 3, I). We denote this conflict set as C . Note that for the occludee (the occluded object), some of the feature vectors in the occluded region are mis-classified as foreground, however, their likelihoods are lower compared to those of the occluder at the same pixel (indicated by the intensity of the color).

   139	We exploit this by taking into account the relationship between both objects. In particular, we assign the feature vector f i to one of the two objects by comparing their foreground likelihoods (Figure 3, II):

   148	
   149	Order recovery. At this stage, each pixel has been updated independently. However, it is natural to assume that in the overlapping region of two bounding boxes only one object is the foreground object, whereas the other object is in the background. Hence all pixels should be assigned to either of the two objects. To encode this property we estimate the occlusion order between the objects (Figure 3, IV). Specifically, we estimate the occlusion order R ( I 1 , I 2 ) by comparing the number of pixels assigned to each object in the region of the segmentation conflict:
   150	
   151	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   152	
   153	Figure 4 illustrates the effectiveness of this approach at recovering the occlusion order, even in challenging, multiobject occlusion scenarios. Using the predicted occlusion order R ( I 1 , I 2 ) , we reassign the visibility variables z i,n in al 'all or nothing' manner, such that all the variables that are not assigned to the outlier model are assigned to the object in the front. Figure 3 illustrates how the multi-object occlusion reasoning benefits the instance segmentation compared to the input segmentation which was achieved by processing the images independently.
   154	
   155	Self-correction through occlusion updates. Compared to the occlusion variables Z m , which were estimated in the feed-forward stage, the newly estimated visibility variables z i,n take into account the knowledge of neighboring objects and their occlusion order graph. This newly acquired knowledge can subsequently be used to recompute the model likelihood p ( F | Θ y ) of the occluded object, by replacing the occlusion variables in Equation 7. As our experimental results demonstrate, this top-down refinement enables CompositionalNets to correct miss-classifications that were induced by wrongly estimated occlusion vari- ables. This will particularly improve the classification performance of occluded objects by a large margin. We repeat the self-correction through multi-object reasoning recurrently as the updated classification score can lead to changes in the assignment of the mixture models, and hence can lead to improved segmentations.

Occlusion levels L1-L3 are defined as: L1: 1%-30%, L2: 30%-60%, L3: 60%-90% of the object is occluded.

   169	
   170	## 4. Experiments
   171	
   172	We evaluate our deep network for multi-instance segmentation under occlusion on the KINS dataset and on an artificially generated occlusion challenge dataset. We will present experimental results of weakly-supervised modal and amodal instance segmentation, and ablate the occlusion reasoning module for order recovery.
   173	
   174	## 4.1. Datasets
   175	
   176	KINS. The KINS dataset [24] is augmented from KITTI [9] with more instance pixel-level annotation for 8 categories including amodal instance segmentation and relative occlusion order. Amodal instance segmentation aims at segmenting the complete instance shape, even when the object is only partially visible. The dataset contains 7474 images for training and 7517 for testing.
   177	
   178	Occlusion Challenge. The amodal segmentation predicted on 2D real-world images by human judgements is still subjective and imprecise. Synthetic datasets are created to generate pixel-accurate annotations for the invisible parts of objects. Some generate 2D images from synthetic 3D scenes, e.g., DYCE [6] provides natural configuration of objects in indoor scenes and SAIL-VOS[15] provides densely labeled video data extracted from the photo-realistic game GTA-V. These datasets contain natural object boundaries, while being deficient in photo-realistic textures. Others like [19] superimposing objects over other images to create arti- ficial occlusion with real-world textures. For the purpose of studying different types of occlusion challenges, we introduce a dataset with custom artificially generated occlusion scenarios. We crop non-occluded objects from images in KITTI based on their segmentation mask, and place them in images with random backgrounds (Figure 5).
   179	
   180	Figure 6: Qualitative results for modal segmentation on KINS and images from our occlusion challenge. The top row show the input images including bounding box annotations. Images in the second row are generated by the baseline CompNet, and the third row shows the results by our CompNet with multi-object ORM. The last row shows the ground truth.
   181	
   182	<!-- image -->
   183	
   184	Since only complete visible objects are selected and the exact shape of each object is available, our occlusion challenge provides more accurate annotation for amodal masks compared with the human estimated masks in KINS. Most importantly, the synthetic nature of the dataset allows us to design challenging scenarios occlusion scenarios. In particular, we propose three types of occlusion challenges: 1) The basic and simplest occlusion scenario includes two objects, where one occludes the other. 2) A much more complex occlusion relationship is defined when four objects occlude each other with different amounts partial occlusion. Recovering the occlusion order and modal as well as amodal segmentation requires significant reasoning processes, even for humans. 3) Another challenging scenario is defined when the occluders contain a mixed set of object classes, some of which are known at training time, while are some are natural objects that are not part of the training data, such as street signs, bushes, and etc.
   185	
   186	## 4.2. Implementation Details
   187	
   188	Baselines. We implement Mask R-CNN[11] as baseline method for the modal instance segmentation. We further compare to CompositionalNets [28] with and without our proposed multi-object reasoning module. We apply multi-object reasoning either with one reasoning iteration (iter=1) or recurrently with two iterations (iter=2). Note that the CompositionalNets perform segmentation in a weaklysupervised manner from bounding box annotations only. We compare our method with BBTP [14] and PCNet-M
   189	
   190	[35]. BBTP uses a bounding box tightness prior to perform weakly-supervised instance segmentation using boxlevel annotations. PCNet-M performs amodal mask completion in a self-supervised manner. PCNet-M is trained to recover the amodal mask with a given artificially occluded modal mask. In contrast, our model predicts amodal masks with bounding box supervision only and is capable of handling both known and unknown occluder classes.
   191	
   192	Training setup. We follow the training strategy as proposed in [16, 28]. CompositionalNets are trained from the feature activations of a ResNeXt-50 [12] model that is pretrained on ImageNet[4] and fine-tuned on the respective datasets. We set the number of mixture components to M = 8 . Wetrain for 60 epochs using SGD with momentum r = 0 . 9 and a learning rate of lr = 0 . 01 .
   193	
   194	## 4.3. Instance segmentation under Occlusion
   195	
   196	Modal segmentation. We report modal instance segmentation performance in the top Tabulars in Table 1 on KINS and Table 2 on our occlusion challenge. Four occlusion levels of objects are defined as: L0: 0%-1%, L1: 1%-30%, L2: 30%-60%, L3:60%-90% of the object area being occluded. To prevent the performance from being affected by a poor bounding box prediction, all models are given the ground truth amodal bounding boxes during training and testing. For the KINS data, we observe that the fully supervised method outperforms weakly-supervised methods. However, our proposed multi-object extension with occlusion reasoning manages to significantly reduce the gap between weakly supervised methods and the fully supervised baseline. We outperform the baseline CompNet performance in every occlusion level, especially in higher occlusion levels by L2((9.6%) and L3 (11.3%) in terms of mIoU . We observe similar performance patterns on the data for our occlusion challenge. While the CompNet per- forms similarly for the first and third occlusion challenge, its performance drops significantly when four objects mutually occlude each other compared to the other two scenarios. Our multi-object occlusion reasoning module enables CompNets to close this performance gap. Overall, the multi-object reasoning improves the segmentation results in all occlusion levels and for all challenge scenarios, and in particular for high occlusion levels L2 and L3.
   197	
   198	Table 2: Modal and amodal instance segmentation on our occlusion challenge (top and bottom respectively). We compare to fully-supervised Mask R-CNN, PCNet-M, and weakly-supervised BBTP, and CompNets with and without ORM. Occlusion levels L0-L3 are defined as: L0: 0%-1%, L1: 1%-30%, L2: 30%-60%, L3: 60%-90% of the object are are occluded. Comparison between different times of occlusion reasoning iteration is also reported. Note that PCNet-M by design cannot handle unknown occlusion, and therefore cannot be applied in the last challenge.

   223	Amodal segmentation. We report amodal instance segmentation in the bottom Tabulars in Table 1 and Table 2. Note that the self-supervised PCNet-M requires the modal mask as supervision to learn amodal mask completion. From the results, we observe that our model outperforms all other weakly-supervised methods in all levels of occlusion on the KINS data as well as in the occlusion challenge. We even surpass the mask-supervised PCNet-M in overall performance by 6.5% in mIoU .
   224	
   225	In summary, with the ability of reasoning about multiobject occlusion, our proposed ORM significantly improves the robustness to occlusion compared with primary CompositionalNet. It achieves accurate instance segmentation in challenging occlusion scenarios (Figure 6. Our weakly- supervised model even outperforms mask-supervised methods in terms of amodal instance segmentation.
   226	
   227	## 4.4. Ablation study
   228	
   229	In Table 3, we verify the effectiveness of the order recovery by evaluating modal and amodal segmentation results on our occlusion challenge. We perform experiments without pair-wise order (NOD), and with our predicted pairwise order (OD). The results demonstrate the benefit of the order recovery, since per pixel competition cannot always correctly indicate the occluder and the occludee.
   230	
   231	## 5. Conclusion
   232	
   233	In this paper, we introduced a deep network for multiobject instance segmentation that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from bounding box supervision only. In particular, our network defines a generative model of multiple objects and achieves enhanced robustness through reasoning about multi-object occlusion. We further extended our architecture with an occlusion reasoning module that enables efficient inference in generative models with multiple objects. In particular, it detects erroneous feed-forward predictions and and corrects them through reasoning about the occlusion order of objects. Our experiments demonstrate the robustness of our proposed deep network for instance segmentation under occlusion on the KITTI INstance dataset and a dataset with synthetic occluders.

content: 1 # [Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion [CVPR 2021]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.02107) 2 3 4 5 6 ### Abstract 7 8 Analyzing complex scenes with DNN is a challenging task, particularly when images contain multiple objects that partially occlude each other. Existing approaches to image analysis mostly process objects independently and do not take into account the relative occlusion of nearby objects. We propose a deep network for **multi-object instance segmentation** that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from **bounding box supervision only**. 9 10 We also introduce an **Occlusion Challenge** dataset generated from real-world segmented objects with accurate annotations and propose a taxonomy of occlusion scenarios that pose a particular challenge for computer vision. 11 12 13 14 15 ------------ 16 ### NOTICE 17 dataset links and model will be released in a few days. Update: 18 June 18 19 20 ### Requirments 21 The code uses Python 3.6 and it is tested on PyTorch GPU version 1.2, with CUDA-10.0 and cuDNN-7.5. 22 23 ### Installation 24 1. Clone the repository with: 25 ``` 26 git clone https://github.com/XD7479/Multi-Object-Occlusion.git 27 cd Multi-Object-Occlusion 28 ``` 29 2. Install requirments: 30 ``` 31 pip install -r requirements.txt 32 ``` 33 ### Datasets 34 1. Download the KINS dataset here and the Occlusion Challenge dataset here. 35 2. Enter the project folder and make links for the datasets: 36 ``` 37 ln -s kins 38 ln -s occ_challenge 39 ``` 40 3. Download the pre-trained model here. 41 4. Make links for the pre-trained model: 42 ``` 43 ln -s models 44 ``` 45 7. Check the configuration file `configs.py` for the dataset and backbone you're using: 46 ``` 47 dataset_eval = 'occ_challenge' # kins, occ_challenge 48 nn_type = 'resnext' # vgg, resnext 49 50 ``` 51 9. Run the evaluation code with: 52 ``` 53 python3 eval_meanIoU.py 54 ``` 55 56 ----------- 57 ### Segmentation Demo 58 59 60 61 62 ### Citation 63 64 ``` 65 @misc{yuan2021robust, 66 title={Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion}, 67 author={Xiaoding Yuan and Adam Kortylewski and Yihong Sun and Alan Yuille}, 68 booktitle = {Proceedings IEEE Conf. on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)}, 69 month = jun, 70 year = {2021}, 71 month_numeric = {6} 72 } 73 ``` 74 75 ### Contact 76 If you have any questions you can contact Xiaoding Yuan by xiaodingyuan.tj@gmail.com. 77 78 79

   117	def get_mixture_models(dim_reduction=True, tag='_it2'):
   118	    FG_Models = []
   119	    FG_prior = []
   120	    CNTXT_Models = []
   121	    CNTXT_prior = []
   122	    for category in categories['train']:
   123	        try:
   124	            load_path = meta_dir + 'init_{}/mix_model_vmf_pascal3d+_EM_all_context{}/mmodel_{}_K{}_FEATDIM512_{}_specific_view_{}.pickle'.format(nn_type, tag, category, K, layer, context_cluster)
   125	            alpha, beta, prior = np.load(load_path, allow_pickle=True)
   126	        except:
   127	            print('Failed to load Mixture Model: {}'.format(category.upper()))
   128	            FG_Models.append(None)
   129	            FG_prior.append(None)
   130	            CNTXT_Models.append(None)
   131	            CNTXT_prior.append(None)
   132	            continue
   133	
   134	        mix_fg = np.array(alpha)
   135	        mix_context = np.array(beta)
   136	        prior_fg = np.array(prior)[:, 0, :, :]
   137	        prior_context = np.array(prior)[:, 1, :, :]
   138	
   139	        # Reduce dimensions of the mixture model since most of the boundary regions only sampled one or two images during model building
   140	        if dim_reduction:
   141	            old_dim = prior_fg.shape
   142	            prior_whole = prior_fg + prior_context
   143	            h_cut=1
   144	            w_cut=1
   145	            while np.sum(prior_whole[:, h_cut:-h_cut, :].reshape(-1, 1)) / np.sum(prior_whole.reshape(-1, 1)) > 0.995:
   146	                h_cut += 1
   147	
   148	            while np.sum(prior_whole[:, :, w_cut:-w_cut].reshape(-1, 1)) / np.sum(prior_whole.reshape(-1, 1)) > 0.995:
   149	                w_cut += 1
   150	
   151	            mix_fg = mix_fg[:, :, w_cut:-w_cut, h_cut:-h_cut]
   152	            mix_context = mix_context[:, :, w_cut:-w_cut, h_cut:-h_cut]
   153	            prior_fg = prior_fg[:, h_cut:-h_cut, w_cut:-w_cut]
   154	            prior_context = prior_context[:, h_cut:-h_cut, w_cut:-w_cut]
   155	            new_dim = prior_fg.shape
   156	
   157	            print('Dim Reduction - {}: ({}, {}) --> ({}, {})'.format(category, old_dim[1], old_dim[2], new_dim[1], new_dim[2]))
   158	
   159	
   160	        mix_fg = np.transpose(mix_fg, [0, 1, 3, 2])
   161	        mix_context = np.transpose(mix_context, [0, 1, 3, 2])
   162	
   163	
   164	        # dealing with empty kernels                                  mix_fg.shape = [8, 512, H, W]
   165	        mix_fg = np.transpose(mix_fg, [2, 3, 0, 1])                 # mix_fg.shape = [H, W, 8, 512]
   166	        zero_map = (np.sum(mix_fg, axis=3) == 0)
   167	        vc_num = mix_fg.shape[3]
   168	        avg_feature = mix_fg.reshape(-1, vc_num).sum(0)
   169	        avg_feature = avg_feature / np.sum(avg_feature)
   170	        mix_fg[zero_map] = avg_feature
   171	        mix_fg = np.transpose(mix_fg, [2, 3, 0, 1])
   172	
   173	        #dealing with empty kernels                                   mix_context.shape = [8, 512, H, W]
   174	        mix_context = np.transpose(mix_context, [2, 3, 0, 1])       # mix_context.shape = [H, W, 8, 512]
   175	        zero_map = (np.sum(mix_context, axis=3) == 0)
   176	        vc_num = mix_context.shape[3]
   177	        avg_feature = mix_context.reshape(-1, vc_num).sum(0)
   178	        avg_feature = avg_feature / np.sum(avg_feature)
   179	        mix_context[zero_map] = avg_feature
   180	        mix_context = np.transpose(mix_context, [2, 3, 0, 1])
   181	
   182	        # dealing with empty kernels                                   prior_fg.shape = [8, H, W]
   183	        prior_fg[prior_fg == 0] = np.min(prior_fg[prior_fg > 0])
   184	
   185	        # dealing with empty kernels                                   prior_context.shape = [8, H, W]
   186	        prior_context[prior_context == 0] = np.min(prior_context[prior_context > 0])
   187	
   188	
   189	        mix_fg = torch.from_numpy(mix_fg).type(torch.FloatTensor)
   190	        FG_Models.append(mix_fg.cuda(device_ids[0]))
   191	
   192	        mix_context = torch.from_numpy(mix_context).type(torch.FloatTensor)
   193	        CNTXT_Models.append(mix_context.cuda(device_ids[0]))
   194	
   195	        prior_fg = torch.from_numpy(prior_fg).type(torch.FloatTensor)
   196	        FG_prior.append(prior_fg.cuda(device_ids[0]))
   197	
   198	        prior_context = torch.from_numpy(prior_context).type(torch.FloatTensor)
   199	        CNTXT_prior.append(prior_context.cuda(device_ids[0]))
   200	
   201	
   202	    return [FG_Models, FG_prior, CNTXT_Models, CNTXT_prior]

   210	              Clutter_Models=get_clutter_models(),

   212	
   213	    return net.cuda(device_ids[0])

   624	    def place_mask_in_image(self, mask, mask_dim_in_img, center_in_img, org_img_shape, empty_fill=-1):
   625	        segmentation = np.ones(org_img_shape) * empty_fill
   626	
   627	        mask_as_img = cv2.resize(mask, (int(mask_dim_in_img[1]), int(mask_dim_in_img[0])),
   628	                                 interpolation=cv2.INTER_NEAREST)
   629	
   630	        mask_h, mask_w = mask_as_img.shape
   631	
   632	        top_img, left_img, bottom_img, right_img = [math.floor(center_in_img[0] - mask_h / 2),
   633	                                                    math.floor(center_in_img[1] - mask_w / 2),
   634	                                                    math.floor(center_in_img[0] + mask_h / 2),
   635	                                                    math.floor(center_in_img[1] + mask_w / 2)]
   636	        top_mask, left_mask, bottom_mask, right_mask = [0, 0, mask_h, mask_w]
   637	
   638	        if top_img < 0:
   639	            top_mask += 0 - top_img
   640	            top_img = 0
   641	
   642	        if left_img < 0:
   643	            left_mask += 0 - left_img
   644	            left_img = 0
   645	
   646	        if bottom_img > segmentation.shape[0]:
   647	            bottom_mask += segmentation.shape[0] - bottom_img
   648	            bottom_img = segmentation.shape[0]
   649	
   650	        if right_img > segmentation.shape[1]:
   651	            right_mask += segmentation.shape[1] - right_img
   652	            right_img = segmentation.shape[1]
   653	
   654	        if bottom_mask - top_mask != bottom_img - top_img:
   655	            height = min(bottom_mask - top_mask, bottom_img - top_img)
   656	            bottom_mask = top_mask + height
   657	            bottom_img = top_img + height
   658	
   659	        if right_mask - left_mask != right_img - left_img:
   660	            width = min(right_mask - left_mask, right_img - left_img)
   661	            right_mask = left_mask + width
   662	            right_img = left_img + width
   663	
   664	        segmentation[top_img:bottom_img, left_img:right_img] = mask_as_img[top_mask:bottom_mask, left_mask:right_mask]
   665	
   666	        return segmentation
   667	
   668	    def add_fg_prior(self, per_pixel_cls, prior, thrd=0.5):
   669	        per_pixel_cls += (per_pixel_cls == 0).type(torch.FloatTensor) * (prior > thrd).type(torch.FloatTensor)
   670	        return per_pixel_cls

   672	    def binarize_pixel_cls(self, pixel_cls, pixel_cls_score, target_label=1):
   673	        obj_resp = pixel_cls_score * (pixel_cls == target_label).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   674	        non_obj_resp = pixel_cls_score * (pixel_cls != target_label).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   675	
   676	        return obj_resp - non_obj_resp

   678	    def segment_per_pixel_cls(self, x, category, k_max, clutter_weight=1.0, occ_prior_score=None):
   679	        '''
   680	        Segmentation by per-pixel classification of foreground ("1"), context ("2"), and occluder ("0")
   681	        :param fg_match:            image patch explained by foreground model
   682	        :param bg_match:            image patch explained by context model
   683	        :param clutter_match:       image patch explained by clutter model
   684	        :return:                    per_pixel_cls containing labels and per_pixel_cls_score containing log prob ratios --> [H, W, 1]
   685	        '''
   686	
   687	        ###     Generate Foreground Activation
   688	        fg_model = self.center_crop(self.fg_models[category], x.shape[2:])
   689	        fg_prior = self.center_crop(self.fg_prior[category], x.shape[2:])
   690	        fg_match = torch.log(fg_prior[k_max] * (x * fg_model[k_max]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
   691	        # fg_match = torch.log((x * fg_model[k_max]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
   692	
   693	        ###     Generate Background Activation
   694	        context_model = self.center_crop(self.context_models[category], x.shape[2:])
   695	        context_prior = 1 - fg_prior
   696	        bg_match = torch.log(context_prior[k_max] * (x * context_model[k_max]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
   697	        # bg_match = torch.log((x * context_model[k_max]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
   698	
   699	        ###     Generate Outlier Activation
   700	        clutter_match = torch.log(self.clutter_conv1o1(x, norm=False) + 1e-6)
   701	        clutter_match, _ = clutter_match.max(1)
   702	        # clutter_match = torch.log(fg_prior[k_max] * torch.exp(clutter_match) + 1e-6) * clutter_weight
   703	        clutter_match = torch.log((torch.exp(clutter_match) + 1e-6) * clutter_weight)
   704	
   705	        ###     Generate Counter Activations
   706	        # TODO
   707	        # context_b = (bg_match > 0).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   708	        context_b = (bg_match - clutter_match > 0).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   709	        fg_counter_match = clutter_match * (1 - context_b) + bg_match * context_b
   710	
   711	        # fg_b = (fg_match > 0).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   712	        fg_b = (fg_match - clutter_match > 0).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   713	        bg_counter_match = clutter_match * (1 - fg_b) + fg_match * fg_b
   714	
   715	        fg_b = (fg_match - bg_match > 0).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   716	        clutter_counter_match = bg_match * (1 - fg_b) + fg_match * fg_b
   717	
   718	        ###     per-pixel classification of foreground, context, and occluder
   719	        per_pixel_cls = torch.zeros(fg_match.shape)  # occluder
   720	        per_pixel_cls[fg_match > fg_counter_match] = 1  # object foreground

   722	        per_pixel_cls[bg_match > bg_counter_match] = 2  # context background

   724	        per_pixel_cls_score = (clutter_match - clutter_counter_match) * (per_pixel_cls == 0).type(
   725	            torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0]) \
   726	                              + (fg_match - fg_counter_match) * (per_pixel_cls == 1).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(
   727	            device_ids[0]) \
   728	                              + (bg_match - bg_counter_match) * (per_pixel_cls == 2).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(
   729	            device_ids[0])

   731	        if occ_prior_score is not None:
   732	            per_pixel_cls[occ_prior_score > 0] = 0
   733	            per_pixel_cls_score[occ_prior_score > 0] = occ_prior_score[occ_prior_score > 0]

   737	    def predict_amodal_box(self, box, obj_center_on_model, fg_prior, factor, image_size):
   738	        '''
   739	        Predict Amodal box: amodal completion on the bounding box level
   740	        :param box:                     the partial bounding box information
   741	        :param obj_center_on_model:     the center obtained from sldiing window
   742	        :param fg_prior:                the foreground mask prior of the given object/pose
   743	        :param factor:                  scaling factor to obtain the new patch
   744	        :param image_size:              the size of the image
   745	        :return:                        the estiamted amodal bounding box
   746	        '''
   747	
   748	        model_h, model_w = fg_prior.shape
   749	        restore_factor = feat_stride / factor
   750	
   751	        inmodal_ctr = np.array([(box[2] + box[0]) / 2, (box[3] + box[1]) / 2])
   752	        inmodal_size = np.array([box[2] - box[0], box[3] - box[1]])
   753	
   754	        off_set = np.array(
   755	            [model_h / 2 - obj_center_on_model[0], model_w / 2 - obj_center_on_model[1]]) * restore_factor
   756	
   757	        true_ctr = off_set + inmodal_ctr
   758	
   759	        relative_true_ctr = off_set + inmodal_size / 2
   760	
   761	        pred_amodal_height = max(abs(relative_true_ctr[0] - 0), abs(relative_true_ctr[0] - inmodal_size[0])) * 2
   762	        pred_amodal_width = max(abs(relative_true_ctr[1] - 0), abs(relative_true_ctr[1] - inmodal_size[1])) * 2
   763	
   764	        visible_obj_x, visible_obj_y = np.where(fg_prior > 0.5)
   765	
   766	        mean_amodal_height = (np.max(visible_obj_x) - np.min(visible_obj_x)) * restore_factor
   767	        mean_amodal_width = (np.max(visible_obj_y) - np.min(visible_obj_y)) * restore_factor
   768	
   769	        if pred_amodal_height < mean_amodal_height and pred_amodal_width < mean_amodal_width:
   770	            pred_amodal_height = mean_amodal_height
   771	            pred_amodal_width = mean_amodal_width
   772	
   773	        tlx = max(true_ctr[0] - pred_amodal_height / 2, 0)
   774	        tly = max(true_ctr[1] - pred_amodal_width / 2, 0)
   775	        brx = min(tlx + pred_amodal_height, image_size[0])
   776	        bry = min(tly + pred_amodal_width, image_size[1])
   777	
   778	        amodal_box = np.array([tlx, tly, brx, bry])
   779	        return amodal_box

   781	    def predict_amodal_box_old(self, pred_score_map, box, obj_center_on_model, fg_prior, factor, image_size):
   782	        '''
   783	        Predict Amodal box: amodal completion on the bounding box level
   784	        :param pred_score_map:          the foreground score map of the region
   785	        :param box:                     the partial bounding box information
   786	        :param obj_center_on_model:     the center obtained from sldiing window
   787	        :param fg_prior:                the foreground mask prior of the given object/pose
   788	        :param factor:                  scaling factor to obtain the new patch
   789	        :param image_size:              the size of the image
   790	        :return:                        the estiamted amodal bounding box
   791	        '''
   792	        patch_center = np.array([(box[2] + box[0]) / 2, (box[3] + box[1]) / 2])
   793	        model_h, model_w = fg_prior.shape
   794	        restore_factor = feat_stride / factor
   795	
   796	        visible_obj_x, visible_obj_y = np.where(pred_score_map > 0)
   797	
   798	        if visible_obj_x.shape[0] == 0 and visible_obj_y.shape[0] == 0:
   799	            visible_obj_x, visible_obj_y = np.where(fg_prior > 0.7)
   800	
   801	        amodal_height = 2 * max(np.max(visible_obj_x) - model_h / 2,
   802	                                model_h / 2 - np.min(visible_obj_x)) * restore_factor
   803	        amodal_width = 2 * max(np.max(visible_obj_y) - model_w / 2,
   804	                               model_w / 2 - np.min(visible_obj_y)) * restore_factor
   805	
   806	        off_set = np.array(
   807	            [model_h / 2 - obj_center_on_model[0], model_w / 2 - obj_center_on_model[1]]) * restore_factor
   808	        center_in_img = patch_center + off_set
   809	
   810	        tlx = max(center_in_img[0] - amodal_height / 2, 0)
   811	        tly = max(center_in_img[1] - amodal_width / 2, 0)
   812	        brx = min(tlx + amodal_height, image_size[0])
   813	        bry = min(tly + amodal_width, image_size[1])
   814	
   815	        amodal_box = np.array([tlx, tly, brx, bry])
   816	        return amodal_box

   838	    def get_vc_activation_with_binary_fg_mask(self, x, gt_category, use_context_center=False, use_mixture_model=False,
   839	                                              context_thrd=None, mmodel_thrd=None, bmask_post_process=False,
   840	                                              cntxt_pad=0):
   841	        '''
   842	        Perform foreground disentanglement given an image patch x, used for building Context-Aware CompNets
   843	        :param x:                   the image patch image patch - bbox short side resize to 224
   844	        :param gt_category:         the ground truth label of x
   845	        :param use_context_center:  boolean that indicates if should use context center to segment foreground
   846	        :param use_mixture_model:   boolean that indicates if should use mixture model to segment foreground
   847	        :param context_thrd:        float thrd that determines the binary mask for context center
   848	        :param mmodel_thrd:         float thrd that determines the binary mask for mixture model
   849	        :return:                    a binary mask as a pixel-level classification of foreground in the feature map --> [H, W] numpy array
   850	        '''
   851	        assert use_context_center ^ use_mixture_model
   852	
   853	        with torch.no_grad():
   854	
   855	            x = self.extractor(x)
   856	
   857	            if use_context_center:
   858	                assert context_thrd != None
   859	
   860	                context_resp = self.get_context_response(x, category=gt_category)
   861	
   862	                binary_fg_mask = (context_resp <= context_thrd).type(torch.FloatTensor)
   863	
   864	                x = self.vc_conv1o1(x)
   865	                x = self.exp(x)
   866	
   867	            if use_mixture_model:
   868	                assert mmodel_thrd != None
   869	
   870	                x = self.vc_conv1o1(x)
   871	                x = self.exp(x)
   872	
   873	                fg_model = self.center_crop(self.fg_models[gt_category], x.shape[2:])
   874	                fg_prior = self.center_crop(self.fg_prior[gt_category], x.shape[2:])
   875	                context_model = self.center_crop(self.context_models[gt_category], x.shape[2:])
   876	                context_prior = 1 - fg_prior
   877	
   878	                clutter_match = torch.log(self.clutter_conv1o1(x, norm=False) + 1e-6)
   879	                clutter_match = clutter_match[:, 0, :, :]
   880	
   881	                k_max, _ = self.get_best_mixture(x, clutter_match, category=gt_category)
   882	
   883	                fg_match = torch.log(fg_prior[k_max] * (x * fg_model[k_max]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
   884	                bg_match = torch.log(context_prior[k_max] * (x * context_model[k_max]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
   885	                clutter_match = torch.zeros(fg_match.shape).cuda(device_ids[0])
   886	
   887	                context_b = (bg_match - clutter_match > 0).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   888	                baseline = clutter_match * (1 - context_b) + bg_match * context_b
   889	
   890	                fg_resp = fg_match - baseline
   891	
   892	                binary_fg_mask = (fg_resp > mmodel_thrd).type(torch.FloatTensor)
   893	
   894	            binary_fg_mask = torch.squeeze(binary_fg_mask)
   895	
   896	            if bmask_post_process:
   897	                self.binary_mask_post_process(binary_fg_mask, cntxt_pad=cntxt_pad)
   898	
   899	            return x.cpu().detach().numpy().squeeze(), binary_fg_mask.cpu().detach().numpy()

   901	    def get_context_response(self, x, category):
   902	        '''
   903	        Obtain the cos similarity between x, layer feature, and context centers
   904	        :param x:           layer feature of image patch
   905	        :param category:    categroy of x (could be predict or ground truth)
   906	        :return:            max_p ( cos similarity between x and context centers ) --> [H, W] torch tensor on GPU
   907	        '''
   908	        with torch.no_grad():
   909	            context_resp = self.context_conv1o1(x)
   910	            context_resp = context_resp[:, category * context_cluster: (category + 1) * context_cluster, :, :]
   911	            context_resp, _ = context_resp.max(1)
   912	
   913	        return context_resp

   915	    def get_best_mixture(self, x, clutter_match, category, clutter_weight=1.0, occ_prior=None):
   916	        '''
   917	        Find the best mixture that models x, given label
   918	        :param x:           vMF activation of image patch
   919	        :param category:    categroy of x (could be predict or ground truth)
   920	        :param clutter_weight: weight of clutter match competing fg and bg
   921	        :return:            integer indicating the best mixture
   922	        '''
   923	
   924	        fused_model = self.center_crop(self.fused_models[category], x.shape[2:])
   925	        fg_match = torch.log((x * fused_model).sum(1) + 1e-6)
   926	
   927	        if occ_prior is None:
   928	            occ_prior = torch.zeros(fg_match.shape).type(torch.BoolTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
   929	
   930	        assert occ_prior.shape[1:] == fg_match.shape[1:], 'occ_prior shape does not match'
   931	
   932	        not_occ = ((~occ_prior) * ((fg_match - clutter_match * clutter_weight) > 0)).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(
   933	            device_ids[0])
   934	
   935	        view_point_scores = (not_occ * (fg_match - clutter_match * clutter_weight)).sum((1, 2))
   936	        k_max = int(torch.argmax(view_point_scores).item())
   937	
   938	        return k_max, view_point_scores[k_max]

   940	    def binary_mask_post_process(self, bmask, obj_prior='rigid', cntxt_pad=0):
   941	
   942	        h, w = bmask.shape
   943	
   944	        if cntxt_pad > 0:
   945	            bmask[0:cntxt_pad, :] = 0
   946	            bmask[:, 0:cntxt_pad] = 0
   947	            bmask[h - cntxt_pad:h, :] = 0
   948	            bmask[:, w - cntxt_pad:w] = 0
   949	
   950	        if obj_prior == 'rigid':
   951	            for i in range(h):
   952	                for j in range(w):
   953	
   954	                    if bmask[i][j] == 1:
   955	                        continue
   956	
   957	                    if torch.any(bmask[0:i, j] == 1) and torch.any(bmask[i + 1:, j] == 1) and torch.any(
   958	                            bmask[i, 0:j] == 1) and torch.any(bmask[i, j + 1:] == 1):
   959	                        bmask[i][j] = 1

   961	    def calc_mask_iou(self, pred_mask, gt_mask):
   962	        return np.sum(pred_mask * gt_mask) / np.sum((pred_mask + gt_mask >= 1.).astype(float))

   964	    def cv2_mask_post_process(self, pixel_cls_score, filtering=True, iou_converge_thrd=0.95, gaussian_mask_size=33):
   965	        refined_pixel_cls_score = pixel_cls_score
   966	        cur_median = np.median(refined_pixel_cls_score)
   967	        old_pixel_cls = refined_pixel_cls_score
   968	
   969	        if not filtering:
   970	            return old_pixel_cls
   971	
   972	        refined_pixel_cls_score = cv2.GaussianBlur(refined_pixel_cls_score, (gaussian_mask_size, gaussian_mask_size), 0)
   973	        refined_pixel_cls_score -= np.median(refined_pixel_cls_score) - cur_median
   974	        refined_pixel_cls = refined_pixel_cls_score
   975	
   976	        while self.calc_mask_iou(old_pixel_cls, refined_pixel_cls) < iou_converge_thrd:
   977	            old_pixel_cls = refined_pixel_cls
   978	            refined_pixel_cls_score = cv2.GaussianBlur(refined_pixel_cls_score,
   979	                                                       (gaussian_mask_size, gaussian_mask_size), 0)
   980	            refined_pixel_cls_score -= np.median(refined_pixel_cls_score) - cur_median
   981	            # refined_pixel_cls = (refined_pixel_cls_score > 0).astype(int)
   982	
   983	        return refined_pixel_cls

   985	    def slide_window_on_model(self, x, fused_model, clutter_match, stride=1, occ_prior=None, clutter_weight=1.0):
   986	        '''
   987	        Helper method: slide the vMF activation along the mixture model to look for the region of the mixture model that explains the activation the best
   988	        :param x:               vMF activation of image patch
   989	        :param fused_model:     the model that combines both fg and context model into one
   990	        :param clutter_match:   clutter response on the same image patch
   991	        :param stride:          sliding window step size
   992	        :param occ_prior:      classify with an occlusion prior, used in post-processing after reasoning multi occlusion
   993	        :param clutter_weight: weight of clutter competing bg and fg
   994	
   995	        :return:                [ max_value, center of the mmodel that offers best alignment, best mixture (pose) ]
   996	        '''
   997	        best_val = -1
   998	        best_center = [-1, -1]
   999	        best_k = -1
  1000	
  1001	        heat_map = np.zeros((fused_model.shape[2] - x.shape[2] + 1, fused_model.shape[3] - x.shape[3] + 1))
  1002	        k_max_map = np.ones((fused_model.shape[2] - x.shape[2] + 1, fused_model.shape[3] - x.shape[3] + 1)) * -1
  1003	
  1004	        for h in range(0, fused_model.shape[2] - x.shape[2] + 1, stride):
  1005	            for w in range(0, fused_model.shape[3] - x.shape[3] + 1, stride):
  1006	
  1007	                # fg_match = torch.log((1 - omega) * fg_prior[:, h:h + x.shape[2], w:w + x.shape[3]] * (x * fg_model[:, :, h:h + x.shape[2], w:w + x.shape[3]]).sum(1) + omega * context_prior[:, h:h + x.shape[2], w:w + x.shape[3]] * ( x * context_model[:, :, h:h + x.shape[2], w:w + x.shape[3]]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
  1008	                fg_match = torch.log((x * fused_model[:, :, h:h + x.shape[2], w:w + x.shape[3]]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
  1009	
  1010	                if occ_prior is None:
  1011	                    occ_prior = torch.zeros(fg_match.shape).type(torch.BoolTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
  1012	
  1013	                assert occ_prior.shape[1:] == fg_match.shape[1:], 'occ_prior shape does not match'
  1014	                not_occ = ((~occ_prior) * ((fg_match - clutter_match * clutter_weight) > 0)).type(
  1015	                    torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
  1016	                # not_occ = ((fg_match - clutter_match) > 0).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(device_ids[0])
  1017	
  1018	                view_point_scores = (not_occ * (fg_match - clutter_match * clutter_weight)).sum((1, 2))
  1019	
  1020	                k_max = torch.argmax(view_point_scores).item()
  1021	
  1022	                heat_map[h][w] = view_point_scores[k_max]
  1023	                k_max_map[h][w] = k_max
  1024	
  1025	                if view_point_scores[k_max] > best_val:
  1026	                    best_val = view_point_scores[k_max]
  1027	                    best_center = [h + int(x.shape[2] / 2), w + int(x.shape[3] / 2)]
  1028	                    best_k = k_max
  1029	
  1030	        if stride <= 1:
  1031	            return best_val, np.array(best_center), best_k
  1032	        else:
  1033	            thrd = np.percentile(heat_map[k_max_map >= 0], 75)
  1034	
  1035	        for h in range(0, fused_model.shape[2] - x.shape[2] + 1):
  1036	            for w in range(0, fused_model.shape[3] - x.shape[3] + 1):
  1037	
  1038	                if k_max_map[h][w] >= 0:
  1039	                    score = heat_map[h][w]
  1040	                    k_max = k_max_map[h][w]
  1041	                else:
  1042	                    if heat_map[h - h % stride][w - w % stride] > thrd:
  1043	
  1044	                        fg_match = torch.log((x * fused_model[:, :, h:h + x.shape[2], w:w + x.shape[3]]).sum(1) + 1e-6)
  1045	
  1046	                        not_occ = ((fg_match - clutter_match * clutter_weight) > 0).type(torch.FloatTensor).cuda(
  1047	                            device_ids[0])
  1048	
  1049	                        view_point_scores = (not_occ * (fg_match - clutter_match * clutter_weight)).sum((1, 2))
  1050	
  1051	                        k_max = torch.argmax(view_point_scores).item()
  1052	                        score = view_point_scores[k_max]
  1053	                    else:
  1054	                        continue
  1055	
  1056	                if score > best_val:
  1057	                    best_val = score
  1058	                    best_center = [h + int(x.shape[2] / 2), w + int(x.shape[3] / 2)]
  1059	                    best_k = k_max
  1060	
  1061	        return best_val, np.array(best_center), best_k

  1063	    def center_crop(self, model, dim):
  1064	        '''
  1065	        Helper method: crop the input model into the input dim with respect to the model's center
  1066	        :param model:   model that needs cropping
  1067	        :param dim:     cropping dimension
  1068	        :return:        cropped model
  1069	        '''
  1070	
  1071	        axis_num = len(model.shape)
  1072	        h_crop, w_crop = dim
  1073	
  1074	        if axis_num == 2:
  1075	            h, w = model.shape
  1076	
  1077	            if h_crop > h or w_crop > w:
  1078	                diff = int((max(h_crop - h, w_crop - w) + 1) / 2)
  1079	                model = F.pad(model, (diff, diff, diff, diff), 'constant', 0)
  1080	                h, w = model.shape
  1081	
  1082	            assert h_crop <= h and w_crop <= w
  1083	            return model[int((h - h_crop) / 2): int((h + h_crop) / 2), int((w - w_crop) / 2): int((w + w_crop) / 2)]
  1084	
  1085	        if axis_num == 3:
  1086	            _, h, w = model.shape
  1087	
  1088	            if h_crop > h or w_crop > w:
  1089	                diff = int((max(h_crop - h, w_crop - w) + 1) / 2)
  1090	                model = F.pad(model, (diff, diff, diff, diff, 0, 0), 'constant', 0)
  1091	                _, h, w = model.shape
  1092	
  1093	            assert h_crop <= h and w_crop <= w
  1094	            return model[:, int((h - h_crop) / 2): int((h + h_crop) / 2), int((w - w_crop) / 2): int((w + w_crop) / 2)]
  1095	
  1096	        if axis_num == 4:
  1097	            _, _, h, w = model.shape
  1098	
  1099	            if h_crop > h or w_crop > w:
  1100	                diff = int((max(h_crop - h, w_crop - w) + 1) / 2)
  1101	                model = F.pad(model, (diff, diff, diff, diff, 0, 0, 0, 0), 'constant', 0)
  1102	                _, _, h, w = model.shape
  1103	
  1104	            assert h_crop <= h and w_crop <= w
  1105	            return model[:, :, int((h - h_crop) / 2): int((h + h_crop) / 2),
  1106	                   int((w - w_crop) / 2): int((w + w_crop) / 2)]
  1107	
  1108	        if axis_num < 2 or axis_num > 4:
  1109	            print('center crop operation is only supported for axis_num = [2, 4].')

  1111	    def fuse_model(self, fg_model, fg_prior, context_model, context_prior, omega):
  1112	        '''
  1113	        Helper method: generate fused model based on input omega
  1114	        :param fg_model:        Foreground object model
  1115	        :param fg_prior:        Foreground object prior
  1116	        :param context_model:   Context model
  1117	        :param context_prior:   Context prior
  1118	        :param omega:           omega used to determine context influence when merging fg and context model
  1119	        :return:                return fused singular model per category per mixture
  1120	        '''
  1121	        # Fused foreground and context templates via priors and omega value
  1122	        if fg_model == None:
  1123	            return None
  1124	
  1125	        fused_model = (1 - omega) * fg_model * fg_prior.unsqueeze(1) + omega * context_model * context_prior.unsqueeze(
  1126	            1)

13:Analyzing complex scenes with Deep Neural Networks is a challenging task, particularly when images contain multiple objects that partially occlude each other. Existing approaches to image analysis mostly process objects independently and do not take into account the relative occlusion of nearby objects. In this paper, we propose a deep network for multi-object instance segmentation that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from bounding box supervision only. Our work builds on Compositional Networks, which learn a generative model of neural feature activations to locate occluders and to classify objects based on their non-occluded parts. We extend their generative model to include multiple objects and introduce a framework for efficient inference in challenging occlusion scenarios. In particular, we obtain feed-forward predictions of the object classes and their instance and occluder segmentations. We introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that locates erroneous segmentations and estimates the occlusion order to correct them. The improved segmentation masks are, in turn, integrated into the network in a top-down manner to improve the image classification. Our experiments on the KITTI INStance dataset (KINS) and a synthetic occlusion dataset demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our model at multi-object instance segmentation under occlusion. Code is publically available at https://github.com/XD7479/Multi-Object-Occlusion.
27:In this paper, we introduce a deep network for multiobject instance segmentation that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from bounding box supervision only. Our work builds on and significantly extends CompositionalNets. Specifically, we extend the generative model in CompositionalNets to allow for instance segmentation of multiple mutually occluding objects in an image. This multiobject generative model is hard to optimize because of the mutual dependencies between objects. To solve this optimization efficiently, we introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that takes as input the independent predictions of each objects label, the instance segmentation and the occluder segmentation (Figure 1). We proceed to estimate possibly erroneous predictions through an occlusion voting mechanism. During occlusion voting, each object in the image votes for every pixel in its bounding box if the pixel is occupied by the object or if is occluded. Pixels which receive ambiguous votes from multiple objects indicate segmentation errors. To correct these we leverage the occlusion order of overlapping bounding boxes based on the classification scores. The corrected instance and occlusion segmentation masks are fed back into the CompositionalNet to mask out those features that induced segmentation errors, and to improve the prediction of the object class.
190:[35]. BBTP uses a bounding box tightness prior to perform weakly-supervised instance segmentation using boxlevel annotations. PCNet-M performs amodal mask completion in a self-supervised manner. PCNet-M is trained to recover the amodal mask with a given artificially occluded modal mask. In contrast, our model predicts amodal masks with bounding box supervision only and is capable of handling both known and unknown occluder classes.
233:In this paper, we introduced a deep network for multiobject instance segmentation that is robust to occlusion and can be trained from bounding box supervision only. In particular, our network defines a generative model of multiple objects and achieves enhanced robustness through reasoning about multi-object occlusion. We further extended our architecture with an occlusion reasoning module that enables efficient inference in generative models with multiple objects. In particular, it detects erroneous feed-forward predictions and and corrects them through reasoning about the occlusion order of objects. Our experiments demonstrate the robustness of our proposed deep network for instance segmentation under occlusion on the KITTI INstance dataset and a dataset with synthetic occluders.

73:Figure 3: Detailed method in Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM). The input of ORM is the segmentation likelihood maps of a pair of neighboring objects. The map contains pixel-level prediction into foreground (blue pixels), background (green pixels) and occlusion (red pixels). Brighter pixel refers to higher likelihood on each position. Segmentation conflict is detected when one pixel is defined as foreground for both objects. Pixel-level competition is performed to solve the conflict and re-assign pixels. Pair-wise occlusion order recovery results from the competition, and the occludee's likelihood map is then updated accordingly.
